title: Return on Tokens (ROT)
gist: Co-written with Poetic founder Markie Wagner, the essay argues 'tokenmaxxing' (maximizing AI token spend as a proxy for value) is a market delusion now breaking, and reframes enterprise AI returns as Return on Tokens (ROT = (value of output - token cost)/token cost). Its core thesis: Agents are the wrong architecture for most repetitive economic work; AI should act as a compiler that converts business processes into cheap deterministic code, not as a runtime that improvises forever.

themes.theme: Tokenmaxxing as the cycle's dumb metric

insights.claim: Enterprises have been maximizing AI token spend as a proxy for value ('tokenmaxxing'), creating an incentive cascade from boards to employees where nobody is rewarded for questioning whether the spend produces useful output.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: If token spend is decoupled from value, lab consumption revenue is structurally fragile and could reprice sharply once enterprises start measuring output instead of usage.
insights.quote: The market incentivized companies to spend tokens, so boards incentivized leaders to spend tokens, so leaders incentivized managers to spend tokens, so managers incentivized employees to spend tokens. Nobody had an incentive to say that the tokens aren't doing useful stuff.

insights.claim: Lab revenue went parabolic in part because Anthropic and OpenAI switched from subscription to consumption-based pricing just as tokenmaxxing peaked, leaving customers no time to prepare for usage-based bills.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Consumption-pricing-driven revenue spikes may be a one-time pull-forward; watch for deceleration as enterprises optimize usage rather than a durable growth rate.
insights.quote: All of this happened, by the way, right as the labs switched from subscription-based to consumption-based revenue models, so companies had no time to prepare. It is no wonder token usage, and therefore lab revenue, went parabolic.

insights.claim: Every market cycle elevates a flawed proxy metric — railroad miles in the 1800s, eyeballs in the dot-com era, top-line gross revenue in the 2010s (WeWork) — and this cycle's equivalent is token consumption.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Pattern-match AI to prior proxy-metric bubbles: the underlying asset can still be valuable, but valuations pinned to the proxy (tokens) rather than returns are vulnerable to a spell-break.
insights.quote: Every cycle has its dumb metric.
insights.locator: Every cycle has its dumb metric

insights.claim: Uber broke the tokenmaxxing spell: its CTO said the company burned its entire 2026 Claude Code token budget by April, and in May its COO said justifying AI spend was getting harder because the link between consumption and shipped features 'is not there yet.'
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A marquee, sophisticated buyer publicly questioning ROI is a leading indicator of broader enterprise pullback on token budgets — a demand-side risk for the labs.
insights.quote: Its CTO said that the company had burned through its 2026 Claude Code token budget by April. In May, its COO said that the company was having a harder time justifying its AI spend, because the link between AI consumption and shipped features “is not there yet.”
insights.locator: It took Uber to break the spell

insights.claim: Even AI vendors and bulls are conceding the waste: Sam Altman called it a 'huge issue,' Palantir's Alex Karp likened tokenmaxxing to 'a porn addiction,' Ramp's Veeral Patel called it the 'Token Casino,' and Amazon shut down its AI leaderboard.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: When even the sellers admit waste, the narrative tide is turning toward measured ROI — a sentiment shift that favors efficiency vendors over raw-consumption providers.
insights.quote: Ramp’s Veeral Patel called it the Token Casino: “useful software wrapped in mechanics that make spend feel like progress. It starts with the oldest trick in the book: abstract the money.”
insights.locator: the consultant saying his client accidentally burned half a billion

themes.theme: Cost side: routing and the rise of cheap Chinese models

insights.claim: The first cost lever enterprises are pulling is 'routing' — using frontier US models only for hard reasoning and running most workloads on ~99% cheaper Chinese open-source models, with Brian Armstrong estimating 80% of workloads will run on 99%-cheaper models within 12-18 months.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Workload migration to cheap open models compresses frontier-lab pricing power on the bulk of demand; monitor OpenRouter rankings as a real-time gauge of the shift.
insights.quote: 80% of workloads will be running on 99% cheaper models within 12-18 months
insights.locator: Now that the spell has been broken, cooler heads

insights.claim: The shift toward Chinese models on OpenRouter appeared in lockstep with consumption-based pricing, signaling that usage-based billing directly drives buyers to optimize model cost.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Pricing model changes have second-order effects on model market share; consumption billing accelerates commoditization of inference.
insights.quote: The move to Chinese models actually showed up in lockstep with consumption-based pricing
insights.locator: You can see this in the OpenRouter AI Model Rankings

themes.theme: Agents are the wrong architecture for most work

insights.claim: Agents structurally cannot deliver the accuracy real economic work requires: they improvise fresh each run, so for repetitive high-stakes tasks like fraud detection or underwriting, 80% accuracy is effectively 0% usable.
insights.speaker: Markie Wagner
insights.so_what: Distinguish AI use cases by accuracy tolerance: prototyping/dashboards tolerate Agents, but core revenue processes do not — a key filter for which AI bets actually monetize.
insights.quote: For new features, prototypes, or dashboards, 80% accuracy is fine. For the real repetitive work on which the economy runs, like fraud detection or underwriting decisions, 80% accuracy is 0% usable.
insights.locator: Agents Aren't It (For Most Work) Either

insights.claim: Enterprise AI has failed where coding succeeded because most process work lives as ~3,000 tacit rules in employees' heads far from SF engineers; AI can only evolve what it can touch.
insights.speaker: Markie Wagner
insights.so_what: The moat in enterprise AI is the ground-game capture of tacit knowledge, not model quality — favoring on-site implementation companies over pure API providers.
insights.quote: AI can only evolve what it can touch, which is why it’s been great at coding but has largely failed to do useful things in the enterprise.
insights.locator: Engineers don't know what to build

insights.claim: Without explicit goals to hill-climb against, agent-generated (or human) code decays into slop because there is no purifying force to evaluate good vs bad, and open-ended agents drive token spend without value — a fast track to negative ROT.
insights.speaker: Markie Wagner
insights.so_what: Deployments lacking measurable objectives are the highest-risk for negative ROI; the presence of explicit goals/eval is a diligence signal for AI projects.
insights.quote: Without a goal to hill-climb against, code (whether written by humans or generated by Agents) decays into slop in the limit because there’s no purifying force to evaluate what’s good and bad.
insights.locator: The original sin is that there are no goals

themes.theme: AI as compiler, not runtime (the core framework)

insights.claim: The central reframe: Agents should replace the software company (the expensive, rare 'thinking' that compiles goals into code), not the software itself (the cheap, perpetual 'doing'); run deterministic code forever and only invoke AI when the rules change.
insights.speaker: Markie Wagner
insights.so_what: Reframes AI economics as CapEx (one-time compile) not OpEx (perpetual inference) — implying durable token demand is far lower than consumption-revenue extrapolations assume.
insights.quote: Agents should do the thinking, code should do the doing.
insights.locator: AI is a Compiler, Not a Runtime

insights.claim: Current AI implementations run a roughly 1000:1 Thinking-to-Doing Ratio (TDR), reflecting a Silicon Valley assumption that work is mostly thinking when in reality work is mostly doing.
insights.speaker: Markie Wagner
insights.so_what: If TDR collapses toward doing-via-code, per-task inference revenue falls by orders of magnitude — bearish for revenue models priced on continuous agent reasoning.
insights.quote: The current Thinking-Doing Ratio (TDR) in AI implementations is roughly 1000:1, which is not surprising. San Francisco is a Thinking town.
insights.locator: AI is a Compiler, Not a Runtime

insights.claim: Agents are 'the BlackBerry of doing' — a transitional architecture that will not be where most enterprise work happens in five years; that work will run in the deterministic code agents write.
insights.speaker: Markie Wagner
insights.so_what: Positions today's agent-centric AI stack as a soon-to-be-displaced paradigm; long the code-generation/process-capture layer, skeptical of perpetual-runtime agent valuations.
insights.quote: Agents are the BlackBerry of doing.
insights.locator: So we use Agents for Doing

themes.theme: Poetic and the 're-found every business as software' thesis

insights.claim: Poetic (Wagner's company, backed by Founders Fund, Kleiner Perkins, Genius Ventures, and OpenAI) sends mostly ex-Palantir engineers on-site for weeks to extract tacit business rules, compiles them into deterministic code, and claims 100x less token usage with nines of accuracy.
insights.speaker: Markie Wagner
insights.so_what: A vendor explicitly monetizing token-efficiency rather than token-volume is a structural counterweight to the labs; its claimed metrics define the efficiency bar to watch.
insights.quote: The result is 100x less token usage and nines of accuracy on complex tasks. Put differently, each token you spend does 100x more, and it does it right.
insights.locator: Turning Businesses Into Software That Evolves

insights.claim: Poetic is deployed at AIG, SoFi, and Chime, with AIG CEO Peter Zaffino stating it has achieved '99%+ quality outcomes on multi-hour processes' delivering real enterprise value.
insights.speaker: Markie Wagner
insights.so_what: Named blue-chip financial deployments with a CEO accuracy claim are concrete proof points for the deterministic-code approach over improvising agents.
insights.quote: AIG CEO Peter Zaffino said that Poetic has already “achieved 99%+ quality outcomes on multi-hour processes - delivering real enterprise value.”
insights.locator: We are doing it today, for companies like AIG

insights.claim: The decade's defining shift is not 'AI becoming a person' but every business being re-founded as living software that backtests changes in shadow and hill-climbs toward an ideal process, with humans defining goals rather than methods.
insights.speaker: Markie Wagner
insights.so_what: Frames the durable AI value pool as process-as-code transformation of incumbents — incumbents that refactor into code win, those that throw tokens at problems lose.
insights.quote: Everyone thinks the thing that is going to change in the world is that AI is going to become a person, but the real change is that a business is going to become a piece of software.
insights.locator: Everyone thinks the thing that is going to change

insights.claim: Deterministic, in-production process code creates a step-by-step record that makes every process change testable via backtesting, so changes carry little risk and become simple choices for a 'process lead.'
insights.speaker: Markie Wagner
insights.so_what: Backtestable process change is the underrated payoff of code-over-agents — a moat that improvising agents structurally cannot offer.
insights.quote: After months of running, you’ll be able to ask questions like “What if we approved every dispute under $25?” and know in minutes.
insights.locator: To make a change, you have to know what the impact
